title: Price stability
paperName: Cambridge International AS Level Economics (P1 & P2)
summary: 9708 AS inflation and deflation — CPI, demand-pull vs cost-push, and AD–AS analysis with interactive GeoGebra model.
learningObjectives: Calculate inflation rate from CPI data.
Distinguish demand-pull and cost-push inflation on AD–AS.
Evaluate costs of inflation and deflation.

simpleExplanation.title: Rising prices economy-wide
simpleExplanation.summary: Inflation is a sustained increase in the general price level. Moderate inflation is a policy target; hyperinflation destroys money's store of value.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Inflation is like all prices in a supermarket rising together — your shopping basket costs more even if you buy the same items. Deflation is the reverse: the same basket gets cheaper year after year.
steps: CPI tracks basket of goods — inflation rate = ((CPI₁ − CPI₀)/CPI₀) × 100%.
Demand-pull: AD shifts right — 'too much money chasing too few goods'.
Cost-push: SRAS shifts left — oil shock, wage push, import costs.
Deflation: falling P — may increase real debt burden and delay spending.

flashcards.back: A sustained increase in the general price level — not a one-off rise in a single good.
Inflation: P rising. Deflation: P falling. Disinflation: inflation rate falling (prices still rising, but more slowly).
Inflation rate = ((CPI this year − CPI last year) ÷ CPI last year) × 100%
AD increases faster than LRAS capacity — AD shifts right; 'too much money chasing too few goods'; P and Y both rise.
SRAS shifts left due to higher input costs (wages, oil, imports) — P rises but Y falls (stagflation).
Uncertainty, menu costs, shoe-leather costs, redistribution (debtors gain, savers lose), and loss of international competitiveness.

sections.content: **Price stability** (4.6) is a key macro objective alongside low unemployment and growth. Use **separate AD–AS diagrams** for demand-pull vs cost-push — never mix both shifts on one unclear diagram.

Inflation rate = $\frac{\text{CPI}_1 - \text{CPI}_0}{\text{CPI}_0} \times 100$

Real value of money falls as P rises

Purchasing power = $\frac{1}{P}$ (inversely related to price level)

sections.question:
The CPI was 110 in 2023 and 116.6 in 2024.

(a) Calculate the inflation rate.
(b) A worker's nominal wage rose from $30,000 to $31,500. Calculate the real wage change.
(c) Identify whether this is demand-pull or cost-push if AD also rose sharply while oil prices were stable.
sections.solution:
(a) **Inflation rate**
= ((116.6 − 110) ÷ 110) × 100 = **6.0%**

(b) **Real wage change**
Nominal wage increase = ((31,500 − 30,000) ÷ 30,000) × 100 = **5.0%**
Real wage change ≈ 5.0% − 6.0% = **−1.0%** (real wage fell — purchasing power declined)

(c) **Type of inflation**
With **AD rising sharply** and no oil shock → **demand-pull inflation**. AD shifted right beyond productive capacity, pushing P up.

items: **Demand-pull**: AD ↑ → P ↑ and Y ↑ — expansionary policy can worsen inflation near Yf.
**Cost-push**: SRAS ↓ → P ↑ and Y ↓ — contractionary policy may deepen recession.
**Policy dilemma**: cost-push creates stagflation — no easy trade-off.
**Deflation**: falling P increases real debt burden; consumers delay purchases.

sections.question:
An oil price shock increases production costs. On a separate AD–AS diagram:

(a) Show the effect on P and Y.
(b) Explain why contractionary monetary policy may be inappropriate.
(c) Suggest one supply-side response.
sections.solution:
(a) **Diagram**
**SRAS shifts left** from SRAS₁ to SRAS₂. New equilibrium: **higher P**, **lower Y** — stagflation.

(b) **Inappropriate contractionary policy**
Raising interest rates reduces AD further, deepening the **recession** (Y falls more) while only partially offsetting cost-push inflation. The problem is **supply-side**, not excess demand.

(c) **Supply-side response**
Subsidise energy transition, invest in alternative inputs, or negotiate productivity deals — aim to **shift SRAS back right** rather than suppress AD.

sections.content: State the **transmission mechanism**: e.g. "Higher oil prices raise firms' costs → SRAS shifts left → equilibrium P rises from P₁ to P₂." Marks are awarded for linked analysis, not just labels.

steps.caption: Inflation: sustained rise in general price level.
steps.embedHint: Move to higher P on AD–AS — identify inflationary gap.

steps.caption: Demand-pull: AD shifts right; cost-push: SRAS shifts left.
steps.embedHint: Compare two inflation sources on the diagram.

steps.caption: CPI measures inflation using a basket of goods and services.
steps.embedHint: Link diagram P increase to CPI rise in written analysis.

steps.caption: Deflation: falling P — may increase real debt burden.
steps.embedHint: AD falls left — lower P and lower Y (deflationary spiral risk).
